     *// (epoch \- millis) captured once at boot**// ── Forward decl. ─────────────────────────────────*static void connectToMaster();*/\*-------------------------------------------------- \*  SET-UP \*-------------------------------------------------\*/*void setup() {  Serial.begin(115200);  *// I²C \+ IMU*  Wire.begin(SDA\_PIN, SCL\_PIN);  if (\!mpu.begin()) {    Serial.println("MPU6050 NOT found \- halting");    while (true) delay(1000);  }  mpu.setAccelerometerRange(MPU6050\_RANGE\_2\_G);  mpu.setGyroRange         (MPU6050\_RANGE\_250\_DEG);  mpu.setFilterBandwidth   (MPU6050\_BAND\_21\_HZ);  *// GPIO modes*  pinMode(VIBRATION\_PIN, INPUT);  pinMode(TILT\_SENSOR\_PIN, INPUT);  dht.begin();  *// Wi-Fi*  WiFi.begin(ssid, pwd);  while (WiFi.status() \!= WL\_CONNECTED) { delay(500); }  *// Epoch sync (UTC)*  configTime(0, 0, "pool.ntp.org", "time.nist.gov");  while (time(nullptr) \< 24 \* 3600) delay(500);  *// wait for NTP*  epochOffsetMs \= static\_cast\<int64\_t\>(time(nullptr)) \* 1000LL \- millis();  *// TCP*  connectToMaster();}*/\*-------------------------------------------------- \*  MAIN LOOP \*-------------------------------------------------\*/*void loop() {  */\* ── Read discrete sensors ─────────────────── \*/*  float distance   \= analogRead(IR\_SENSOR\_PIN)   / 9.766;  int   vibration  \= digitalRead(VIBRATION\_PIN);  int   tiltRaw    \= digitalRead(TILT\_SENSOR\_PIN);  int   light      \= analogRead(LIGHT\_SENSOR\_PIN);  float tempDHT    \= dht.readTemperature();  float humDHT     \= dht.readHumidity();  */\* ── IMU read (single call) ─────────────────── \*/*  sensors\_event\_t a, g, t;  mpu.getEvent(\&a, \&g, \&t);  */\* ── Compute Euler angles (simple accel-only) ─ \*/*  const float ax=a.acceleration.x, ay=a.acceleration.y, az=a.acceleration.z;  float angleX \= atan2( ay, sqrt(ax\*ax \+ az\*az) ) \* 180.0/PI;  float angleY \= atan2(-ax, sqrt(ay\*ay \+ az\*az)) \* 180.0/PI;  float angleZ \= atan2( az, sqrt(ax\*ax \+ ay\*ay)) \* 180.0/PI;  */\* ── Maintain TCP link ──────────────────────── \*/*  if (\!TCPclient.connected()) {    TCPclient.stop();    connectToMaster();    if (\!TCPclient.connected()) { delay(2000); return; }  }  */\* ── Build JSON payload ─────────────────────── \*/*  StaticJsonDocument\<4096\> doc;  int64\_t nowMs \= millis() \+ epochOffsetMs;  doc\["messageId"\]      \= messageCounter++;  doc\["slaveTimestamp"\] \= nowMs;  doc\["device"\]         \= "ESP32\_Client";  doc\["ID"\]             \= 123;  doc\["status"\]         \= "active";  doc\["distance\_cm"\]    \= distance;  doc\["vibration"\]      \= vibration ? "Detected" : "None";  doc\["tilt"\]           \= tiltRaw ? "tilted" : "not tilted";  doc\["light\_intensity"\]= light;  doc\["accX"\] \= ax; doc\["accY"\] \= ay; doc\["accZ"\] \= az;  doc\["gyroX"\]= g.gyro.x\*180.0/PI;  doc\["gyroY"\]= g.gyro.y\*180.0/PI;  doc\["gyroZ"\]= g.gyro.z\*180.0/PI;  doc\["angleX"\]= angleX; doc\["angleY"\]= angleY; doc\["angleZ"\]= angleZ;  if (isnan(tempDHT) || isnan(humDHT)) {    doc\["temperature"\]="error"; doc\["humidity"\]="error";  } else {    doc\["temperature"\]=tempDHT; doc\["humidity"\]=humDHT;  }  String out;  serializeJson(doc, out);  TCPclient.println(out);  TCPclient.flush();  delay(12000);   *// \-- total loop ≈12 s*}*/\*-------------------------------------------------- \*  HELPER \*-------------------------------------------------\*/*static void connectToMaster() {  if (TCPclient.connect(serverAddress, serverPort)) {    Serial.println("Connected to Master.");  } else {    Serial.println("Master unreachable.");  }} |
| :---- |

*Listing 1 : condensed but complete Day-4 slave firmware (≈ 340 lines before tidy-up).*

---

## **4 | Engineering Decisions**

1. **Single IMU call per loop** – `mpu.getEvent()` fetches accel **and** gyro in one I²C transaction; avoids the double-read latency we saw in earlier sketches.

2. **On-chip digital filtering** – `MPU6050_BAND_21_HZ` balances noise rejection with negligible phase delay for our 12-second cadence.

3. **Epoch-millis offset** – By caching `epochOffsetMs` once, we sidestep `time(nullptr)` every loop (which costs \~50 ms) and still keep millisecond-grade timestamps for RTT calculations.

4. **Memory budget** – With six floats per vector and three vectors (acc, gyro, angle) plus legacy fields, payload stays under **500 B**. The 4 kB `StaticJsonDocument` leaves headroom for later barometer or magnetometer integration.

5. **Loop pacing** – 12 s delay was tuned empirically: it lets the IMU FIFO drain, Wi-Fi remain idle long enough for power save, and still meets our targeted 5-min granularity for cloud analytics once the master merges ten such slaves.

---

## **5 | Validation**

* **Serial traces** confirmed stable readings (acc ≈ ±9.8 m/s² on Z when flat).

* **Master console** received well-formed JSON without fragmentation.

* **Wi-Fi diagnostics** showed no packet drops after an hour-long soak test.

---

## **6 | Impact & Next Steps**

Day-4’s work completes the **sensor front-end** for each slave. We now possess a plug-and-play IMU-enhanced node that the master can treat exactly like the legacy node—no schema changes upstream. The next logical sprint will be to:

1. Fuse accel and gyro via a **complementary filter** on the slave to deliver smooth pitch/roll/yaw.

2. Enable **per-slave rate throttling** so low-priority sensors (e.g., light) publish less frequently than the IMU.

3. Integrate **power profiling** to estimate battery life under the heavier I²C workload.

Overall, the introduction of the MPU-6050 was accomplished **without compromising network reliability, timestamp precision, or payload size**, thereby keeping the edge-to-cloud contract intact.

# Mil7 \- Slave to masterRTT

**Day 6:** 

So what we did on 2nd-3rd june was that we were trying to make 2 models, each model having its own slave and master which would be communicating with AWS IoT core on obviously two separate things. One moder for sir tariq another for me (basil). While doing these configurations we just ran vishal’s code and it gave me errors, which were that my master ESP32 was not able to connect to the AWS IoT core, on further finding i found that it was happening because of my ESP32 not being synchronized correctly, due to which the certificates which are needed for TLS handshake with AWS were not being seen as valid from the master ESP32, So what I had to do was to use NTP which is network time protocol for synchronizing the clock of the master ESP32, once i did that then my ESP32 was able to connect to AWS iot core.

However for Sir, the issue was nothing except the arduino IDE version being not updated. 

Code for that day is below, using NTP for synchronizing the time and hence making possible the TLS handshake. 

![][image4]

SS is attached to show the timing difference if i had not used NTP- \- \- \- \-\> hence no TLS handshake \- \- \- \- \- \> no connection to AWS iot Core.

Codes: 

**Master:** 

\#include "secret.h"  
\#include \<WiFi.h\>  
\#include \<WiFiClientSecure.h\>  
\#include \<PubSubClient.h\>  
\#include \<ArduinoJson.h\>

IPAddress staticIp(192, 168, 1, 184);  
IPAddress gateway(192, 168, 1, 1);  
IPAddress subnet(255, 255, 255, 0);  
IPAddress primaryDNS(8, 8, 8, 8);  
IPAddress secondaryDNS(8, 8, 4, 4);

\#define AWS\_IOT\_PUBLISH\_TOPIC   "esp32/pub"  
\#define AWS\_IOT\_SUBSCRIBE\_TOPIC "esp32/sub"  
\#define SERVER\_PORT 4080

WiFiClientSecure net;  
PubSubClient mqttClient(net);  
WiFiServer TCPserver(SERVER\_PORT);

void messageHandler(char\* topic, byte\* payload, unsigned int length) {  
 Serial.print("Incoming MQTT message on topic: ");  
 Serial.println(topic);

 StaticJsonDocument\<200\> doc;  
 DeserializationError error \= deserializeJson(doc, payload, length);  
 if (error) {  
   Serial.print("deserializeJson() failed: ");  
   Serial.println(error.c\_str());  
   return;  
 }

 const char\* message \= doc\["message"\];  
 Serial.print("Message: ");  
 Serial.println(message);

 }

void connectWiFi() {  
 WiFi.begin(WIFI\_SSID, WIFI\_PASSWORD);  
 Serial.print("Connecting to Wi-Fi");  
 while (WiFi.status() \!= WL\_CONNECTED) {  
   delay(500);  
   Serial.print(".");  
 }  
 Serial.println("\\n WiFi connected");  
 Serial.print("Master ESP IP: ");  
 Serial.println(WiFi.localIP());  
}

void connectAWS() {  
 configTime(0, 0, "pool.ntp.org", "time.nist.gov");  
 Serial.print("Syncing time");  
 while (time(nullptr) \< 100000) {  
   Serial.print(".");  
   delay(500);  
 }  
 Serial.println("\\n Time synced");

 net.setCACert(AWS\_CERT\_CA);  
 net.setCertificate(AWS\_CERT\_CRT);  
 net.setPrivateKey(AWS\_CERT\_PRIVATE);

 mqttClient.setServer(AWS\_IOT\_ENDPOINT, 8883);  
 mqttClient.setCallback(messageHandler);

 Serial.print("Connecting to AWS IoT");  
 while (\!mqttClient.connect(THINGNAME)) {  
   Serial.print(".");  
   delay(1000);  
 }

 if (mqttClient.connected()) {  
   Serial.println("\\n AWS IoT Connected\!");  
   mqttClient.subscribe(AWS\_IOT\_SUBSCRIBE\_TOPIC);  
 } else {  
   Serial.println("\\n AWS IoT Connection Failed\!");  
 }  
}

void publishMessage(StaticJsonDocument\<200\>& doc) {  
 char buffer\[256\];  
 serializeJson(doc, buffer);  
 mqttClient.publish(AWS\_IOT\_PUBLISH\_TOPIC, buffer);  
 Serial.println(" Published to AWS:");  
 Serial.println(buffer);  
}

void handleTCPCommands() {  
 WiFiClient client \= TCPserver.available();  
 if (client) {  
   Serial.println(" Client connected");

   String message \= "";  
   while (client.connected() && client.available()) {  
     char c \= client.read();  
     message \+= c;  
   }

   Serial.println(" Received message:");  
   Serial.println(message);

   StaticJsonDocument\<200\> doc;  
   DeserializationError error \= deserializeJson(doc, message);  
   if (error) {  
     Serial.print(" JSON parse error: ");  
     Serial.println(error.c\_str());  
   } else {  
     publishMessage(doc);  
   }

   client.stop();  
   Serial.println(" Client disconnected\\n");  
 }  
}

void setup() {  
 Serial.begin(115200);  
 Serial.println(" Master ESP Starting...");  
 connectWiFi();  
 connectAWS();  
 TCPserver.begin();  
 Serial.println(" TCP Server started");  
}

void loop() {  
 mqttClient.loop();       // maintain MQTT connection  
 handleTCPCommands();     // receive & forward data  
 delay(1000);             // optional delay  
}

**Slave ESP 32 with NTP (without IMU):**

   
 \#include \<WiFi.h\>  
 \#include \<ArduinoJson.h\>  
 \#include \<DHT.h\>

 // Sensor Pins  
 \#define IR\_SENSOR\_PIN       32  
 \#define VIBRATION\_PIN       33  
 \#define DHT\_PIN             27  
 \#define DHT\_TYPE            DHT11  
 \#define LIGHT\_SENSOR\_PIN    34  
 \#define TILT\_SENSOR\_PIN     15

 DHT dht(DHT\_PIN, DHT\_TYPE);

 // Wi-Fi and TCP Server Config  
 const char\* ssid \= "OPPO F19";               // Your Wi-Fi SSID  
 const char\* password \= "111222333";      // Your Wi-Fi Password  
 const char\* serverAddress \= "192.168.120.68";  // Master ESP32 IP  
 const int serverPort \= 4080;

 WiFiClient TCPclient;

 void setup() {  
   Serial.begin(115200);

   pinMode(VIBRATION\_PIN, INPUT);  
   pinMode(TILT\_SENSOR\_PIN, INPUT);

   dht.begin();

   Serial.println("ESP32: TCP CLIENT \+ Sensor Node");

   WiFi.begin(ssid, password);  
   Serial.print("Connecting to WiFi");  
   while (WiFi.status() \!= WL\_CONNECTED) {  
     delay(500);  
     Serial.print(".");  
   }  
   Serial.println("\\nConnected to WiFi");  
   Serial.print("Slave ESP IP: ");  
   Serial.println(WiFi.localIP());

   connectToMaster();  
 }

 void connectToMaster() {  
   if (TCPclient.connect(serverAddress, serverPort)) {  
     Serial.println("Connected to Master ESP (TCP server)");  
   } else {  
     Serial.println("Failed to connect to Master ESP");  
   }  
 }

 void loop() {  
   // Sensor Readings  
   int irValue \= analogRead(IR\_SENSOR\_PIN);  
   float distance \= irValue / 9.766;  
   int vibValue \= digitalRead(VIBRATION\_PIN);  
   int tiltValue \= digitalRead(TILT\_SENSOR\_PIN);  
   int lightValue \= analogRead(LIGHT\_SENSOR\_PIN);  
   float temp \= dht.readTemperature();  
   float humidity \= dht.readHumidity();

   // Reconnect if TCP client is not connected  
   if (\!TCPclient.connected()) {  
     Serial.println("TCP connection lost. Attempting reconnect...");  
     TCPclient.stop();  
     connectToMaster();  
     if (\!TCPclient.connected()) {  
       Serial.println("Reconnect failed. Retrying...");  
       delay(2000);  
       return;  
     }  
   }

   // JSON Construction  
   StaticJsonDocument\<256\> doc;  
   doc\["device"\] \= "ESP32\_Client";  
   doc\["ID"\] \= 123;  
   doc\["status"\] \= "active";  
   doc\["distance\_cm"\] \= distance;  
   doc\["vibration"\] \= vibValue \== HIGH ? "Detected" : "None";  
   doc\["tilt"\] \= tiltValue \== LOW ? "not tilted" : "tilted";  
   doc\["light\_intensity"\] \= lightValue;

   if (isnan(temp) || isnan(humidity)) {  
     doc\["temperature"\] \= "error";  
     doc\["humidity"\] \= "error";  
   } else {  
     doc\["temperature"\] \= temp;  
     doc\["humidity"\] \= humidity;  
   }

   // Serialize and Send  
   String output;  
   serializeJson(doc, output);  
   TCPclient.println(output);  
   Serial.println("JSON sent:");  
   Serial.println(output);

   TCPclient.flush();  
   delay(12000);  
 }

On 4th june our target was to measure RTT by it we mean that the time it takes the data to get from slave to the AWS IOT core. 

For understanding: 

Slave \- \- \- \- \- \- \> Master \- \- \- \- \- \- \- \> AWS IoT core

On slave we are timestamping each data we send and sending it into the JSON too. Lets say that time is X, then at master once master processes that data and is ready to publish to AWS then we make master to stamp that data too lets say it is Y. So till today we have found out Y-X which is the time it takes the data to originate from slave and go to the master ESP32 right before the publishAWS function takes the command.

So for finding this RTT I used NTP, and millis() function. First of all pasting the codes here which made this Y-X calculation possible:

***Master Esp32 code:***

\#include "secret.h"  
\#include \<WiFi.h\>  
\#include \<WiFiClientSecure.h\>  
\#include \<PubSubClient.h\>  
\#include \<ArduinoJson.h\>  
\#include \<time.h\>

// ──────────  Wi-Fi & AWS Parameters  ──────────  
IPAddress staticIp(192, 168, 1, 184);  
IPAddress gateway(192, 168, 1, 1);  
IPAddress subnet(255, 255, 255, 0);  
IPAddress primaryDNS(8, 8, 8, 8);  
IPAddress secondaryDNS(8, 8, 4, 4);

\#define AWS\_IOT\_PUBLISH\_TOPIC   "esp32/pub"  
\#define AWS\_IOT\_SUBSCRIBE\_TOPIC "esp32/sub"   
\#define SERVER\_PORT             4080

WiFiClientSecure net;  
PubSubClient mqttClient(net);  
WiFiServer TCPserver(SERVER\_PORT);

// ──────────  Batch‐Average Uplink Tracking  ──────────  
bool     uplinkActive     \= false;  
int      uplinkTarget     \= 0;  
int      uplinkCount      \= 0;  
uint64\_t uplinkSum        \= 0;     // sum of one‐way delays (ms)  
int64\_t  masterEpochOffset= 0;     // (epoch\_ms \- millis) at startup

void resetUplinkBatch() {  
 uplinkActive  \= false;  
 uplinkTarget  \= 0;  
 uplinkCount   \= 0;  
 uplinkSum     \= 0;  
}

// ──────────  MQTT callback (for subscribed topics)  ──────────  
void messageHandler(char\* topic, byte\* payload, unsigned int length) {  
 // Not used for uplink delay, but left here if you want esp32/sub  
 Serial.print("Incoming MQTT message on topic: ");  
 Serial.println(topic);  
 StaticJsonDocument\<200\> doc;  
 DeserializationError error \= deserializeJson(doc, payload, length);  
 if (error) {  
   Serial.print("deserializeJson() failed: ");  
   Serial.println(error.c\_str());  
   return;  
 }  
 const char\* msg \= doc\["message"\];  
 Serial.print("Message on esp32/sub: ");  
 Serial.println(msg);  
}

void connectWiFi() {  
 WiFi.begin(WIFI\_SSID, WIFI\_PASSWORD);  
 Serial.print("Connecting to Wi-Fi");  
 while (WiFi.status() \!= WL\_CONNECTED) {  
   delay(500);  
   Serial.print(".");  
 }  
 Serial.println("\\nWi-Fi connected");  
 Serial.print("Master ESP IP: ");  
 Serial.println(WiFi.localIP());  
}

void connectAWS() {  
 // Synchronize to NTP  
 configTime(0, 0, "pool.ntp.org", "time.nist.gov");  
 Serial.print("Syncing time");  
 while (time(nullptr) \< 24 \* 3600) {  
   delay(500);  
   Serial.print(".");  
 }  
 Serial.println("\\nTime synced");

 // Compute epochOffset \= epoch\_ms \- millis()  
 int64\_t nowSec \= time(nullptr);  
 masterEpochOffset \= (nowSec \* 1000LL) \- (int64\_t)millis();  
 Serial.printf("Master epochOffsetMs \= %lld\\n\\n", masterEpochOffset);

 // Set up TLS and MQTT  
 net.setCACert(AWS\_CERT\_CA);  
 net.setCertificate(AWS\_CERT\_CRT);  
 net.setPrivateKey(AWS\_CERT\_PRIVATE);

 mqttClient.setServer(AWS\_IOT\_ENDPOINT, 8883);  
 mqttClient.setCallback(messageHandler);

 Serial.print("Connecting to AWS IoT");  
 while (\!mqttClient.connect(THINGNAME)) {  
   Serial.print(".");  
   delay(1000);  
 }  
 if (mqttClient.connected()) {  
   Serial.println("\\nAWS IoT Connected\!");  
   mqttClient.subscribe(AWS\_IOT\_SUBSCRIBE\_TOPIC);  
 } else {  
   Serial.println("\\nAWS IoT Connection Failed\!");  
 }  
}

void publishMessage(StaticJsonDocument\<300\>& doc) {  
 char buffer\[512\];  
 serializeJson(doc, buffer);  
 mqttClient.publish(AWS\_IOT\_PUBLISH\_TOPIC, buffer);  
 Serial.println("Published to AWS:");  
 Serial.println(buffer);  
}

// ──────────  Handle incoming TCP from Slave  ──────────  
void handleTCPCommands() {  
 WiFiClient client \= TCPserver.available();  
 if (\!client) return;

 Serial.println("TCP client connected");

 // Read JSON (up to '\\n')  
 String message \= "";  
 unsigned long startTime \= millis();  
 while (client.connected()) {  
   if (client.available()) {  
     char c \= client.read();  
     message \+= c;  
     if (c \== '\\n') break;  
   }  
   if (millis() \- startTime \> 500) break;  
 }

 if (message.length() \> 0) {  
   Serial.println("Received message:");  
   Serial.println(message);

   // Capture when we received it (masterReceiveMs)  
   int64\_t masterReceiveMs \= (int64\_t)millis() \+ masterEpochOffset;

   // Parse JSON  
   StaticJsonDocument\<300\> receivedDoc;  
   DeserializationError error \= deserializeJson(receivedDoc, message);  
   if (error) {  
     Serial.print("JSON parse error: ");  
     Serial.println(error.c\_str());  
   } else {  
     uint32\_t msgId \= 0;  
     if (receivedDoc.containsKey("messageId")) {  
       msgId \= receivedDoc\["messageId"\].as\<uint32\_t\>();  
     }

     // Extract the Slave’s absolute timestamp  
     int64\_t slaveEpochMs \= masterReceiveMs;  
     if (receivedDoc.containsKey("slaveTimestamp")) {  
       slaveEpochMs \= receivedDoc\["slaveTimestamp"\].as\<int64\_t\>();  
     }

     // Stamp Master timestamps  
     int64\_t masterSendMs \= (int64\_t)millis() \+ masterEpochOffset;  
     receivedDoc\["masterReceiveTime"\] \= masterReceiveMs;  
     receivedDoc\["masterSendTime"\]    \= masterSendMs;

     // Compute true one‐way “Slave → AWS” delay  
     int64\_t uplinkDelayMs \= masterSendMs \- slaveEpochMs;  
     Serial.printf("Uplink delay for message %u: %lld ms\\n", msgId, uplinkDelayMs);

     // If in batch mode, accumulate  
     if (uplinkActive) {  
       uplinkSum   \+= uplinkDelayMs;  
       uplinkCount \+= 1;  
       if (uplinkCount \>= uplinkTarget) {  
         float avgMs \= (float)uplinkSum / (float)uplinkTarget;  
         float avgS  \= avgMs / 1000.0;  
         Serial.println();  
         Serial.println("=== AVERAGE UPLINK DELAY \===");  
         Serial.printf("Average over %d messages:  %.2f ms   (\~%.3f s)\\n",  
                       uplinkTarget, avgMs, avgS);  
         Serial.println("=============================");  
         Serial.println();  
         resetUplinkBatch();  
       }  
     }

     // Forward to AWS  
     publishMessage(receivedDoc);  
   }  
 }

 client.stop();  
 Serial.println("TCP client disconnected\\n");  
}

// ──────────  Handle “start\_uplink N” on Serial  ──────────  
void handleSerialCommands() {  
 if (\!Serial.available()) return;  
 String cmd \= Serial.readStringUntil('\\n');  
 cmd.trim();

 if (cmd.startsWith("start\_uplink")) {  
   int sp \= cmd.indexOf(' ');  
   int N \= 10;  
   if (sp \> 0) {  
     N \= cmd.substring(sp \+ 1).toInt();  
     if (N \<= 0) N \= 10;  
   }  
   uplinkTarget \= N;  
   uplinkCount  \= 0;  
   uplinkSum    \= 0;  
   uplinkActive \= true;  
   Serial.printf("Started uplink‐delay batch for %d messages\\n\\n", N);  
 }  
 else if (cmd \== "help") {  
   Serial.println("Available commands:");  
   Serial.println("  start\_uplink \[N\]  \- Collect next N uplink delays");  
   Serial.println("  help              \- Show this help\\n");  
 }  
 else {  
   Serial.println("Unknown command. Type 'help' for available commands.\\n");  
 }  
}

void setup() {  
 Serial.begin(115200);  
 Serial.println("Master ESP Starting...");

 // Optionally fix a static IP:  
 // WiFi.config(staticIp, gateway, subnet, primaryDNS, secondaryDNS);

 connectWiFi();  
 connectAWS();  
 TCPserver.begin();  
 Serial.println("TCP Server started on port 4080\\n");  
 Serial.println("Type 'start\_uplink 10' to begin collecting 10 uplink delays\\n");  
}

void loop() {  
 mqttClient.loop();  
 handleTCPCommands();  
 handleSerialCommands();  
 delay(50);  
}

***Slave ESP 32 code:***

| \#include \<WiFi.h\>\#include \<ArduinoJson.h\>\#include \<DHT.h\>\#include \<Wire.h\>\#include \<Adafruit\_MPU6050.h\>\#include \<Adafruit\_Sensor.h\>\#include \<time.h\>\#include \<math.h\>*// ── GPIO map ───────────────────────────────────────*\#define IR\_SENSOR\_PIN    32\#define VIBRATION\_PIN    33\#define DHT\_PIN          27\#define LIGHT\_SENSOR\_PIN 34\#define TILT\_SENSOR\_PIN  15*// I²C pins (default)*\#define SDA\_PIN          21\#define SCL\_PIN          22*// ── Objects ────────────────────────────────────────*DHT               dht(DHT\_PIN, DHT11);Adafruit\_MPU6050  mpu;WiFiClient        TCPclient;*// ── Wi-Fi / upstream ───────────────────────────────*const char \*ssid \= "VINAYRAJ";const char \*pwd  \= "002560112";const char \*serverAddress \= "192.168.100.49";const int   serverPort    \= 4080;*// ── State ──────────────────────────────────────────*uint32\_t messageCounter   \= 1;int64\_t  epochOffsetMs    \= 0;      *// (epoch \- millis) captured once at boot**// ── Forward decl. ─────────────────────────────────*static void connectToMaster();*/\*-------------------------------------------------- \*  SET-UP \*-------------------------------------------------\*/*void setup() {  Serial.begin(115200);  *// I²C \+ IMU*  Wire.begin(SDA\_PIN, SCL\_PIN);  if (\!mpu.begin()) {    Serial.println("MPU6050 NOT found \- halting");    while (true) delay(1000);  }  mpu.setAccelerometerRange(MPU6050\_RANGE\_2\_G);  mpu.setGyroRange         (MPU6050\_RANGE\_250\_DEG);  mpu.setFilterBandwidth   (MPU6050\_BAND\_21\_HZ);  *// GPIO modes*  pinMode(VIBRATION\_PIN, INPUT);  pinMode(TILT\_SENSOR\_PIN, INPUT);  dht.begin();  *// Wi-Fi*  WiFi.begin(ssid, pwd);  while (WiFi.status() \!= WL\_CONNECTED) { delay(500); }  *// Epoch sync (UTC)*  configTime(0, 0, "pool.ntp.org", "time.nist.gov");  while (time(nullptr) \< 24 \* 3600) delay(500);  *// wait for NTP*  epochOffsetMs \= static\_cast\<int64\_t\>(time(nullptr)) \* 1000LL \- millis();  *// TCP*  connectToMaster();}*/\*-------------------------------------------------- \*  MAIN LOOP \*-------------------------------------------------\*/*void loop() {  */\* ── Read discrete sensors ─────────────────── \*/*  float distance   \= analogRead(IR\_SENSOR\_PIN)   / 9.766;  int   vibration  \= digitalRead(VIBRATION\_PIN);  int   tiltRaw    \= digitalRead(TILT\_SENSOR\_PIN);  int   light      \= analogRead(LIGHT\_SENSOR\_PIN);  float tempDHT    \= dht.readTemperature();  float humDHT     \= dht.readHumidity();  */\* ── IMU read (single call) ─────────────────── \*/*  sensors\_event\_t a, g, t;  mpu.getEvent(\&a, \&g, \&t);  */\* ── Compute Euler angles (simple accel-only) ─ \*/*  const float ax=a.acceleration.x, ay=a.acceleration.y, az=a.acceleration.z;  float angleX \= atan2( ay, sqrt(ax\*ax \+ az\*az) ) \* 180.0/PI;  float angleY \= atan2(-ax, sqrt(ay\*ay \+ az\*az)) \* 180.0/PI;  float angleZ \= atan2( az, sqrt(ax\*ax \+ ay\*ay)) \* 180.0/PI;  */\* ── Maintain TCP link ──────────────────────── \*/*  if (\!TCPclient.connected()) {    TCPclient.stop();    connectToMaster();    if (\!TCPclient.connected()) { delay(2000); return; }  }  */\* ── Build JSON payload ─────────────────────── \*/*  StaticJsonDocument\<4096\> doc;  int64\_t nowMs \= millis() \+ epochOffsetMs;  doc\["messageId"\]      \= messageCounter++;  doc\["slaveTimestamp"\] \= nowMs;  doc\["device"\]         \= "ESP32\_Client";  doc\["ID"\]             \= 123;  doc\["status"\]         \= "active";  doc\["distance\_cm"\]    \= distance;  doc\["vibration"\]      \= vibration ? "Detected" : "None";  doc\["tilt"\]           \= tiltRaw ? "tilted" : "not tilted";  doc\["light\_intensity"\]= light;  doc\["accX"\] \= ax; doc\["accY"\] \= ay; doc\["accZ"\] \= az;  doc\["gyroX"\]= g.gyro.x\*180.0/PI;  doc\["gyroY"\]= g.gyro.y\*180.0/PI;  doc\["gyroZ"\]= g.gyro.z\*180.0/PI;  doc\["angleX"\]= angleX; doc\["angleY"\]= angleY; doc\["angleZ"\]= angleZ;  if (isnan(tempDHT) || isnan(humDHT)) {    doc\["temperature"\]="error"; doc\["humidity"\]="error";  } else {    doc\["temperature"\]=tempDHT; doc\["humidity"\]=humDHT;  }  String out;  serializeJson(doc, out);  TCPclient.println(out);  TCPclient.flush();  delay(12000);   *// \-- total loop ≈12 s*}*/\*-------------------------------------------------- \*  HELPER \*-------------------------------------------------\*/*static void connectToMaster() {  if (TCPclient.connect(serverAddress, serverPort)) {    Serial.println("Connected to Master.");  } else {    Serial.println("Master unreachable.");  }} |
| :---- |

***Explaining how we calculated RTT from slave to master:***

In this system, the goal is to measure the time it takes for a data packet to travel from the Slave ESP32, reach the Master ESP32, and then be sent to AWS IoT Core. This is not a full round-trip time, but a one-way uplink delay, measuring how long it takes the data to travel in the direction from the slave to the cloud. This delay is calculated by measuring two time points: one when the data is created on the slave, and one when the master is about to publish it to AWS. The difference between these two gives us the delay.

Now, both the slave and master ESP32s don’t have real-time clocks built in. They rely on the internal `millis()` timer, which simply counts milliseconds since the device booted. This is fine for measuring durations within a single device, but not enough for comparing timestamps between two devices, because they boot at different times and their `millis()` counters are not synchronized.

To solve this, both devices first synchronize their clocks using NTP (Network Time Protocol). This allows each device to get the current time in seconds since the Unix epoch (January 1, 1970). However, the `time()` function used for this only gives the time in seconds, and we need millisecond precision to get accurate delay measurements. So, after synchronizing with NTP, each device calculates an epoch offset, which is the difference between the current epoch time (converted to milliseconds) and the current `millis()` value.

For example, the slave does this:

`slaveEpochOffset = (nowSec * 1000LL) - (int64_t)millis();`

This means: “What is the real-world Unix time right now, minus the time the device has passed since it booted?” That offset can then be used later to convert any `millis()` value into a real timestamp by simply adding the offset:

`(int64_t)millis() + slaveEpochOffset`

This same logic is applied on the master as well, which calculates its own `masterEpochOffset` after doing its own NTP sync.

With that setup, the slave periodically reads sensor data, generates a timestamp using:

`int64_t nowEpochMs = (int64_t)millis() + slaveEpochOffset;`

and adds this timestamp to a JSON payload as `slaveTimestamp`. This marks the exact time the data was created, in Unix epoch milliseconds. The message is then sent over TCP to the master ESP32.

On the master side, when a TCP message is received, the master first records the time of reception using the same method:

`int64_t masterReceiveMs = (int64_t)millis() + masterEpochOffset;`

This tells when the master got the message, although this value is not directly used in the RTT calculation, it can be used later when finding the RTT till AWS maybe. The master then parses the JSON message and extracts the `slaveTimestamp` that was included by the slave. This value is the original timestamp from the slave, we’ll call it X.

Just before the master is about to publish this data to AWS IoT Core, it records another timestamp because we know that time keeps increasing doesn't stops:

`int64_t masterSendMs = (int64_t)millis() + masterEpochOffset;`

We’ll call this Y. This marks the time at which the message is about to leave the master on its way to AWS.

Now the actual uplink delay — the total time from when the slave created the data to when the master is sending it to AWS — is simply the difference between these two timestamps:

`uplinkDelayMs = masterSendMs - slaveEpochMs;`

This gives a clear, accurate measurement of how much time has passed between the generation of data on the slave and its transmission to AWS from the master. This delay includes the time it took to send the data over TCP from slave to master, any internal processing or queuing delay at the master, and any small time gap in preparing the data for AWS transmission.

The reason this works accurately is because both devices are synchronized to the same global time reference (NTP), and the offset between `millis()` and real-world time is properly applied on both ends. That’s why we used `millis()` in combination with `+ epochOffset`  to turn it into an absolute timestamp and why earlier during NTP sync, that offset is calculated using a subtraction: `(epoch_now_ms - millis())`.

# Mil8 \- RTT & Lambda Setup

## **Project Background and Context**

The overarching goal of our AWS IoT project is to design a scalable, efficient, and secure data communication architecture between multiple embedded devices (sensors) and the AWS Cloud. The motivation stems from the need to manage sensor data from multiple nodes (slave devices) by first aggregating it at a local coordinator (master ESP32), and then forwarding that consolidated data to AWS IoT Core using the MQTT protocol. This method is ideal for real-world IoT deployments where a single gateway device manages data from multiple peripherals.

We are using **ESP32 microcontrollers** due to their native Wi-Fi capabilities, high performance, and compatibility with MQTT protocols. Sensor data is acquired by slave devices from multiple modules, passed to the master via TCP, and then relayed securely to AWS.

---

## **Summary of Prior Work (Before Today)**

### **1\. Hardware Setup and Inter-Device Communication**

We configured **two ESP32 boards**, assigning roles as:

* **Slave ESP32**: Connected to multiple sensors including:

  * DHT (temperature and humidity),

  * IMU (for acceleration and gyroscopic data),

  * Vibration Sensor,

  * Tilt Sensor, and

  * IR Distance Sensor.

* The slave collects readings from all sensors and compiles them into a structured **JSON payload**.

* **Master ESP32**: Acts as a gateway node. It receives the JSON payload from the slave via **TCP Socket communication**. The rationale behind TCP was its reliable delivery over local Wi-Fi.

This architecture lays the foundation for a **multi-slave to single-master network**, scalable for industrial sensor networks.

### **2\. AWS IoT Core Configuration**

We created an **AWS account** and performed the following tasks:

* Registered the **ESP32 thing** in **AWS IoT Core**.

* Generated and attached certificates for secure TLS communication.

* Created an **MQTT topic** `esp32/pub`, which serves as the entry point for the master ESP32 to publish data.

To verify communication, we used the **AWS MQTT Test Client** to subscribe to `esp32/pub` and validated that the payloads were being received when master ESP32 published.

### **3\. Timestamp Synchronization**

We added:

* A timestamp of **when the slave sends data**, known as `slaveTimestamp`.

* A timestamp of **when the master receives the data**, known as `server_timestamps`.

This dual-timestamp approach was implemented to:

* Measure communication latency between the devices.

* Eventually calculate full **Round Trip Time (RTT)** when AWS timestamp is also included.

---

## **Progress Made**

Today was a significant milestone in the project, as we **implemented end-to-end data enrichment and round-trip latency calculation using AWS services.** Below is a detailed explanation of each task:

---

### **1\. IoT Rule to Capture AWS Server Timestamp**

To compute full communication latency, we needed to capture the **timestamp when AWS IoT Core receives the MQTT message** from the master ESP32.

To do this:

We defined an **IoT Rule** in AWS IoT Core.

The SQL-based rule was configured with a statement like:  
`SELECT *, timestamp() AS server_timestamps FROM 'esp32/pub'`

This rule **automatically appends the AWS reception timestamp** (`server_timestamps`) to the incoming MQTT message.

After appending the timestamp, the rule **forwards the enriched message** to a new topic called:

`esp32/timeServer`

This acts as an intermediate topic that temporarily holds enriched data before it is further processed.

![][image5]  
---

### **2\. Lambda Function for RTT Calculation**

Next, we created a **Lambda function** with the following responsibilities:

#### **Trigger Setup**

* The Lambda function is triggered whenever a new message is published to the topic `esp32/timeServer`.

#### **Payload Processing Logic**

* Inside the `lambda_handler`, the function performs:

  * **Extraction of slaveTimestamp**: Timestamp embedded originally by the slave ESP32.

  * **Extraction of server\_timestamps**: AWS timestamp added via the IoT rule.

**RTT Calculation**:  
`RTT = server_timestamps - slaveTimestamp`

This gives us the end-to-end latency from **Slave → Master → AWS IoT Core**.

#### **Payload Cleanup**

* After RTT is computed, we clean up the message by **removing unnecessary timestamps**:

  * `slaveTimestamp`

  * `server_timestamps`

  * (Many other temporary fields)

* The final payload is concise, includes only relevant sensor data and a newly added `RTT` field.

#### **Publishing Back to Final Topic**

The cleaned and enriched data is then **published to another MQTT topic** called:

`esp32/time`

*  This topic is where any consumer (dashboard, logger, or analytics system) can subscribe to receive clean, processed data.

**Final Lambda Code**

|  import jsonimport boto3import time  \# for millisecond timestamp \# Create IoT Data clientiot\_client \= boto3.client(    'iot-data',    region\_name='ap-southeast-2',    endpoint\_url='https://a2xpqfvfr7pva7-ats.iot.ap-southeast-2.amazonaws.com') def lambda\_handler(event, context):    print("Received event:", event)     try:        \# Add millisecond timestamp        enriched\_payload \= event.copy()               slaveTime \= 0        if 'slaveTimestamp' in enriched\_payload:            slaveTime \= enriched\_payload\['slaveTimestamp'\]        elif 'timestamp' in enriched\_payload:            slaveTime \= enriched\_payload\['timestamp'\]         serverTime \= enriched\_payload\['server\_timestamps'\]        enriched\_payload\['RTT'\] \= serverTime \- slaveTime          if 'slaveTimestamp' in enriched\_payload:            enriched\_payload.pop('slaveTimestamp', None)        if 'server\_timestamps' in enriched\_payload:            enriched\_payload.pop('server\_timestamps', None)        if 'masterReceiveTime' in enriched\_payload:            enriched\_payload.pop('masterReceiveTime', None)        if 'masterSendTime' in enriched\_payload:            enriched\_payload.pop('masterSendTime', None)        if 'timestamp' in enriched\_payload:            enriched\_payload.pop('timestamp', None)         \# Convert to bytes for IoT publish        payload\_bytes \= json.dumps(enriched\_payload).encode('utf-8')    except (TypeError, ValueError) as e:        return {            'statusCode': 400,            'body': json.dumps(f"Invalid event format: {str(e)}")        }     try:        \# Publish to topic        response \= iot\_client.publish(            topic='esp32/time',            qos=1,            payload=payload\_bytes        )         return {            'statusCode': 200,            'body': json.dumps(f"Message forwarded to esp32/time with timestamp: {enriched\_payload}")        }     except Exception as e:        return {            'statusCode': 500,            'body': json.dumps(f"Failed to publish message: {str(e)}")        }   |
| :---- |

---

### **3\. IAM Policy Assignment and Lambda Permissions**

Initially, the Lambda function **threw a permission error** when trying to publish to the topic `esp32/time`. This occurred because:

* AWS Lambda by default doesn’t have permission to publish to an MQTT topic.

To resolve this:

We created a **custom IAM Role** with the following permissions:

`{`  
  `"Effect": "Allow",`  
  `"Action": "iot:Publish",`  
  `"Resource": "arn:aws:iot:ap-southeast-2:YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:topic/esp32/time"`  
`}`

* Attached this role to the Lambda function.

After doing so, the function **successfully published enriched data** to `esp32/time`.

---

## **Challenges Faced and Resolved**

| Challenge | Solution |
| ----- | ----- |
| Lambda publish permissions error | Assigned proper IAM policy and added it to Lambda’s execution role |
| Parsing and cleaning payload | Carefully structured the code to handle missing timestamps and prevent key errors |
| Matching timestamps correctly | Used conditional checks to support fallback between `slaveTimestamp` and `timestamp` |

---

## 

## **Current Architecture Overview**

Here’s how data flows now:

`[Sensors]`   
    `↓`

`[Slave ESP32]`  
    `↓ (TCP with timestamp)`

`[Master ESP32]`  
    `↓ (MQTT topic: esp32/pub)`

`[AWS IoT Core Rule]`  
    `↓ (Add AWS timestamp)`

`[esp32/timeServer]`  
    `↓ (Lambda Triggered)`

`[Lambda Function] → Computes RTT → Cleans payload`

    `↓`  
`[esp32/time]`

This structure enables:

* Full pipeline from physical world to cloud.

* Timestamp-based latency monitoring.

* Clean topic for downstream analytics or visualization tools.

---

## 

## **Conclusion**

Today, we achieved a critical milestone in our AWS IoT project by completing an **end-to-end sensor data ingestion, timestamp enrichment, RTT calculation, and cleaned data publishing pipeline.** The integration between ESP32 devices, MQTT, AWS IoT Rules, and Lambda functions is now fully functional, forming a robust foundation for scaling and analysis.

We are now well-positioned to extend the system for data visualization, S3-based storage, and eventually real-time alert systems based on RTT thresholds or sensor anomalies.

This project not only demonstrates effective cloud-IoT integration but also builds skills across embedded systems, secure MQTT communication, cloud function orchestration, and serverless architecture.

# Mil9 \- S3 Setup

## **Background**

This project aims to build a cloud-based architecture for efficiently collecting, enriching, and analyzing sensor data transmitted through a network of ESP32 microcontrollers. The overall objective is to create an edge-to-cloud pipeline that supports reliable sensor communication, timestamp-based latency calculation, serverless data transformation, cloud storage, and real-time visualization.

Previously, we successfully established:

* A dual-node communication model where a **slave ESP32** collects data from multiple sensors and transmits it to a **master ESP32** via TCP.

* The master ESP32 publishes this data to an MQTT topic on **AWS IoT Core**.

* An **IoT Rule** enriches incoming data by adding the AWS reception timestamp.

* A **Lambda function** then calculates the **Round Trip Time (RTT)** between the slave device and the AWS cloud, cleans the data, and publishes the processed payload to another MQTT topic.

Today’s work marks a transition from real-time data transformation to **persistent storage and data visualization**, where the enriched payloads are saved into **Amazon S3** and later accessed for plotting RTT trends using **MATLAB**.

---

## **Summary of Today’s Work**

---

### **1\. Creation of S3 Bucket “iotresearchbucket2”**

We began today’s session by provisioning a new **Amazon S3 bucket** named:

iotresearchbucket2

This bucket is intended to act as the **permanent storage layer** for all enriched JSON payloads received from the sensor network. We designed the folder structure inside the bucket to keep it organized and accessible:

* All sensor data is to be stored inside a subdirectory called `iot-data`.

* Each JSON file saved corresponds to **a single sensor data payload**, identified by its timestamp.

This setup lays the groundwork for:

* Historical data analytics.

* Offline processing using data science tools.

* Serving as a data lake for future machine learning workflows.

---

### **2\. Lambda Permission Update for S3 Access**

After creating the bucket, the next step involved configuring the existing **AWS Lambda function** to **write data into the S3 bucket**. By default, Lambda does not have permissions to access S3, so we did the following:

#### **🛡 IAM Policy Update:**

* Created or updated the IAM Role associated with our Lambda function.

* Attached a policy allowing the function to **put objects into any S3 bucket**:

|  {  "Effect": "Allow",  "Action": "s3:PutObject",  "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::iotresearchbucket2/\*"}  |
| :---- |

This permission was scoped just right: broad enough for testing, but limited to **write-only access** within our bucket to minimize risk.

---

### **3\. Lambda Code Modification for File Upload**

We then revised the Lambda function's code to:

* Take input from the MQTT topic `esp32/timeServer`, which includes the **AWS-added timestamp** and the **slave timestamp**.

* Compute RTT (already done previously).

* Store the final enriched payload into the **S3 bucket** as a `.json` file.

#### **💾 File Naming Convention:**

Each file was saved with a name based on the **timestamp**, for example:

iot-data/data\_2025-06-13T06-20-35-298088Z.json

This helps ensure:

* Uniqueness (no filename collisions).

* Easy chronological sorting.

* Future indexing for time-series queries.

#### **📜 Lambda Code Logic Overview:**

1. Receive event payload triggered by MQTT topic.  
2. Compute RTT using  
3. Clean up the payload to remove `slaveTimestamp`, `server_timestamps and others`.  
4. Generate the file name using `timestamp.`  
5. Upload the final JSON payload to the path: `iot-data/filename.json`.

This was verified by checking the **S3 Management Console**, where the uploaded files were visible with their appropriate content and timestamp-based names.

![][image6]

**Final Code**

|  import jsonimport boto3import timefrom datetime import datetime \# ────────── AWS Clients ──────────s3\_client \= boto3.client('s3') \# ────────── S3 Configuration ──────────BUCKET\_NAME \= 'iotresearchbucket2'  \# Replace with your actual bucket nameKEY\_PREFIX \= 'iot-data/'  \# Optional folder inside your bucket def lambda\_handler(event, context):	print("Received event:", event) 	try:    	\# Copy event to avoid mutation    	enriched\_payload \= event.copy()     	\# ────────── RTT Calculation ──────────    	slaveTime \= 0    	if 'slaveTimestamp' in enriched\_payload:        	slaveTime \= enriched\_payload\['slaveTimestamp'\]    	elif 'timestamp' in enriched\_payload:        	slaveTime \= enriched\_payload\['timestamp'\]     	serverTime \= 0    	if 'server\_timestamps' in enriched\_payload:        	serverTime \= enriched\_payload\['server\_timestamps'\]     	enriched\_payload\['RTT'\] \= serverTime \- slaveTime     	\# ────────── Remove Extra Fields ──────────    	for key in \['slaveTimestamp', 'server\_timestamps', 'masterReceiveTime', 'masterSendTime', 'timestamp'\]:        	if key in enriched\_payload:            	enriched\_payload.pop(key, None)     	\# ────────── Create S3 Object Key ──────────    	timestamp\_str \= datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H-%M-%S-%fZ')    	object\_key \= f"{KEY\_PREFIX}data\_{timestamp\_str}.json"     	\# ────────── Upload to S3 ──────────    	s3\_client.put\_object(        	Bucket=BUCKET\_NAME,        	Key=object\_key,        	Body=json.dumps(enriched\_payload),        	ContentType='application/json'    	)     	return {        	'statusCode': 200,        	'body': json.dumps(f"Data uploaded to S3 as {object\_key}")    	} 	except Exception as e:    	return {        	'statusCode': 500,        	'body': json.dumps(f"Error processing or uploading data: {str(e)}")    	}  |
| :---- |

---

### **4\. Creation of New IAM User and Access Key Generation**

For integrating MATLAB with AWS services, particularly S3, we needed a new IAM user with **programmatic access**.

#### **🔑 Steps Taken:**

1. Created a new IAM user profile.

2. Enabled **programmatic access** by generating:

   * **Access Key ID**

   * **Secret Access Key**

3. Attached the following **permissions** to this user:

|  {  "Effect": "Allow",  "Action": \[    "s3:ListBucket",    "s3:GetObject"  \],  "Resource": \[    "arn:aws:s3:::iotresearchbucket2",    "arn:aws:s3:::iotresearchbucket2/\*"  \]}  |
| :---- |

These permissions ensure that MATLAB can list and read the contents of our bucket without exposing broader administrative access.

---

### **5\. MATLAB Setup for S3 Access and RTT Plotting**

With the S3 bucket now being populated with real sensor data, we shifted to **data visualization using MATLAB**.

#### **🧩 AWS Credentials Configuration:**

MATLAB needs to authenticate with AWS before accessing S3 resources. To achieve this:

* We added the newly created **Access Key** and **Secret Key** to the system’s **environment variables** or MATLAB’s built-in AWS configuration utility.

Within MATLAB directly:

| setenv('AWS\_ACCESS\_KEY\_ID', 'yourAccessKey');setenv('AWS\_SECRET\_ACCESS\_KEY', 'yourSecretKey'); |
| :---- |

#### **📈 MATLAB Code for RTT Plot:**

Using MATLAB’s `fileDatastore` and `jsondecode`, we wrote a script that:

1. Connects to the S3 bucket.

2. Iterates over each JSON file in the `iot-data` directory.

3. Extracts the `RTT` value from each file.

4. Appends the RTTs to an array.

5. Plots RTT over time using:

| plot(rttList, '-o');xlabel('Sample Index');ylabel('RTT (ms)');title('Round Trip Time from Slave ESP32 to AWS');grid on; |
| :---- |

This live plotting setup allows real-time monitoring of network latency, revealing:

* Network delays.

* Packet jitter.

* Performance bottlenecks between edge devices and cloud.

We validated the functionality by running the script and watching the graph get updated as new `.json` files were uploaded to S3.

**Matlab Final Script**

|  % \-- Open a figure once \--
figure;
% TODO: Configure AWS credentials securely (environment variables or AWS config)
% setenv("AWS\_ACCESS\_KEY\_ID", "YOUR_ACCESS_KEY_ID");
% setenv("AWS\_SECRET\_ACCESS\_KEY", "YOUR_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY");
% setenv("AWS\_DEFAULT\_REGION", "your-region");
% \-- Keep track of processed files and their RTTs \--processedFiles \= {};rttList        \= \[\];while true   % Step A: List all .json files under your S3 folder   ds \= datastore("s3://iotresearchbucket2/iot-data/", ...       "Type",             "file", ...       "IncludeSubfolders", true, ...       "FileExtensions",   ".json", ...       "ReadFcn",          @(f) jsondecode(fileread(f)) ...   );   allFiles \= ds.Files;   % Step B: Find which ones are new   newFiles \= setdiff(allFiles, processedFiles);   % Step C: Decode only the new files and append their RTT   for k \= 1:numel(newFiles)       data \= ds.ReadFcn(newFiles{k});   % calls your jsondecode/fileread       rttList(end+1,1) \= data.RTT;      % append       processedFiles{end+1,1} \= newFiles{k};   end   % Step D: Redraw the plot   clf;   plot(rttList, "-o", "LineWidth", 1.5);   grid on;   xlabel("Sample Index");   ylabel("RTT");   title(\["Live RTT (updated " datestr(now,"HH:MM:SS") ")"\]);   drawnow;   % Wait 6 seconds before next pass   pause(6);end  |
| :---- |

---

## **Challenges and Resolutions**

| Challenge | Resolution |
| ----- | ----- |
| Lambda S3 write error | Assigned precise IAM policy allowing `PutObject` to specific bucket |
| File overwrite risk | Used timestamp-based file naming to ensure uniqueness |
| MATLAB credential issues | Properly set AWS credentials using environment variables |
| S3 JSON parsing in MATLAB | Used `jsondecode` inside `fileDatastore` for robust batch processing |

---

## **Updated System Architecture**

Here’s an updated diagram of the current architecture:

| \[Sensors\]    ↓\[Slave ESP32\]   ↓ (TCP with JSON payload) \[Master ESP32\]   ↓ (MQTT to AWS IoT on topic "esp32/pub") \[IoT Rule\] → Add AWS timestamp   ↓\[Topic: esp32/timeServer\]   ↓ (Trigger)\[Lambda Function\]   ↓ Compute RTT   ↓ Save to S3 bucket "iotresearchbucket2/iot-data/"\[MATLAB Script\]   ↓Fetch and Plot RTT over time |
| :---- |

---

## **Future Plans**

Now that we have completed successful integration with S3 and real-time plotting in MATLAB, we plan to proceed with:

1. **Storage Optimization**

   * Add gzip compression for S3 uploads or convert JSON to Parquet in future.

2. **Data Export and Reporting**

   * Export summarized RTT reports (mean, variance) weekly via automated scripts.

3. **Multiple ESP32 Devices**

   * Expand the system to handle multiple slaves and visualize per-device RTT.

---

## **Conclusion**

Today marked a shift from real-time MQTT processing to historical data archiving and visualization. We successfully configured a reliable **end-to-end pipeline** from sensor → MQTT → Lambda → S3 → MATLAB. The use of **AWS-native services** (S3, Lambda, IAM) and **MATLAB as a frontend** offers a highly modular and extensible framework.

This infrastructure now supports:

* Real-time and historical RTT analysis,

* Clean separation of concerns between compute, storage, and visualization,

* Secure and scalable data ingestion with minimal manual effort.

We are now well-positioned to analyze trends, identify performance anomalies, and scale the system for more complex IoT applications.

# Mil10 \- Payload and RTT Analytics

## **Objective**

The focus for this sprint was twofold:

1. **Visualize Round Trip Time (RTT)** using real-time data collected by the ESP32 slave and processed via MATLAB.

2. **Analyze network behavior** using **Wireshark** to inspect packet-level communication between the slave, the master, and AWS IoT Core.

The broader intent was to validate the **accuracy of RTT metrics**, quantify **payload sizes**, and understand **wireless transmission delays** under the given network conditions. This served as an important step toward profiling the performance of our full edge-to-cloud data flow.

---

## **1\. RTT Visualization in MATLAB**

Building on previous days' work where the RTT between the slave device and AWS was computed (using slave timestamp and AWS timestamp injected via Lambda), today's goal was to **plot these RTT values over time in MATLAB**.

### **Process Summary:**

* We retrieved the `.json` files from the **S3 bucket (`iotresearchbucket2`)**.

* Each file contained a single data payload with a calculated `RTT` field in milliseconds.

* Using `fileDatastore` and `jsondecode` in MATLAB, we extracted all RTT values and plotted them using:

plot(rttList, '-o');  
xlabel('Sample Index');  
ylabel('RTT (ms)');  
title('RTT from Slave to AWS over WiFi');  
grid on;

### **📊 Observations:**

* Average RTT: **\~1200 ms (1.2 seconds)**.

* The graph revealed **small fluctuations** in RTT but no packet loss.

* This matched closely with expected delays under standard Wi-Fi latency for MQTT+TLS over 2.4GHz.

---

## **2\. Packet Capture via Wireshark**

To gain deeper insights into packet-level behavior, we attempted to capture traffic between the ESP32 slave and master (local TCP) and from master to AWS (MQTT over TLS). For this, we configured **Wireshark** to act as a monitoring tool in the middle of this data exchange.

### **🔧 Setup Challenge:**

Initially, we were unable to capture traffic because:

* Most modern Wi-Fi interfaces do **not support monitor mode** for third-party packet sniffing on Windows/Linux.

* The built-in Wi-Fi card could not intercept or decode frames from other connected devices.

### **🛠 Solution: Create a Custom Wireless Gateway**

We restructured our network as follows:

* **Created a Wi-Fi hotspot on our laptop** (Linux/Windows machine).

* **Connected both ESP32 boards (master \+ slave)** to this hotspot.

* **Inserted a USB Wi-Fi dongle** to allow the laptop to both:

  * Host the hotspot (AP mode).

  * Remain connected to the internet (client mode) for AWS access.

This effectively made the laptop behave as a **software access point** that sits **in the middle of all traffic**. This configuration allowed **Wireshark** to capture:

* **TCP packets** from **slave ➝ master**

* **MQTT/TLS packets** from **master ➝ AWS endpoint**

---

## **3\. Wireshark Capture & Analysis**

Once traffic interception was working:

### **🔍 Observations:**

* The JSON payloads sent from the slave to the master via TCP were clearly visible.

* MQTT packets heading to AWS were encrypted, but the **packet sizes and timing** were measurable.

* RTT could be visually confirmed by analyzing:

  * `PUSH` packets from slave ➝ master

  * Corresponding `ACK` and upstream MQTT publish from master ➝ AWS

### **📦 Payload Size:**

* Average payload from slave to master: **\~400 bytes**

* Payload contained full sensor data:

  * DHT (temperature \+ humidity)

  * IR Distance

  * Light Intensity

  * Vibration, Tilt

  * IMU Data (accel, gyro, angle)

---

## **4\. Network Speed Benchmark**

To contextualize RTT behavior and payload delivery, we ran a speed test on the same Wi-Fi hotspot network. Results were:

| Metric | Value |
| ----- | ----- |
| Download Speed | **15.37 Mbps** |
| Upload Speed | **14.45 Mbps** |
| RTT (avg) | **\~1.2 seconds** |

This RTT was within acceptable range considering:

* MQTT's overhead

* TLS handshake and encryption latency

* Wi-Fi contention and local TCP delivery

---

## **5\. Key Takeaways**

* 🟢 **RTT values captured by our system match the behavior seen in Wireshark**, confirming the reliability of our time-stamping mechanism.

* 🟢 The custom laptop-turned-router approach using a Wi-Fi dongle was **crucial** for traffic sniffing and should be reused for future network testing.

* 🟢 Payload sizes (\~400 bytes) are small enough for **low-latency MQTT over TLS**, meaning there's headroom to add new sensors or metadata.

* ⚠️ We observed occasional minor jitter, which is typical of Wi-Fi in a home/office setting.

---

## **6\. Next Steps**

* Implement **alerts** in MATLAB if RTT spikes beyond threshold (e.g., 2000 ms).

* Evaluate **packet loss or retransmissions** during longer capture sessions.

* Benchmark performance under different encryption modes or MQTT QoS levels.

* Test the setup under **mobile hotspot conditions** to simulate field deployment.

---

## **Conclusion**

Today's work brought together **MATLAB-based RTT visualization**, **Wireshark-powered packet analysis**, and **network engineering tricks** to establish a controlled testbed for wireless IoT performance testing. By bridging hardware-level communication and software-level analytics, we now have the infrastructure to **track, diagnose, and optimize** every stage of our ESP32-to-AWS pipeline.

This not only ensures our cloud solution is robust, but also arms us with the diagnostic tools needed for future field deployments and real-time debugging.

---

Let me know if you'd like this formatted into a lab notebook entry, PDF report, or a slide summary.

Need to add screenshot of wireshark RTT snapshots

# Mil11 \- DynamoDB from API

This is a **full console-only handbook** that rebuilds our architecture exactly as drawn here and **the five source files with content are listed below** (two writers, two readers, one Node.js HTTP helper).

---

## **0 · Prerequisites & mental map (read once)**

| Thing you need | Why you need it |
| ----- | ----- |
| AWS account with **IoT Core, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB** | The four managed services you will stitch together |
| IAM rights that let you create roles and policies | The console will prompt you for minimal policies as you work |
| ESP32 flashed with MQTT firmware → publishes on **esp32/pub** | Your “left-hand” data source |
| An Amazon EC2 instance running **server.js** (Node/Express) | Your “right-hand” HTTP data source |
| One region (e.g. *ap-southeast-2*) for **every single resource** | Keeps ARNs short and cross-service glue pain-free |

**Naming convention used below**

* MQTT path resources: `iot_data` table, `iotToDynamoWriter` Lambda, `MQTTIngestAPI`, etc.

* HTTP path resources: `iot_HTTP_data` table, `httpToDynamoWriter` Lambda, `HTTPIngestAPI`, etc.  
   Feel free to rename—just stay consistent across code, API Gateway and IoT Rules.

---

## 

![][image7]

## **1 · Create the DynamoDB tables (2 min each)**

1. **Console → DynamoDB → Tables → “Create table”**

2. **Table 1**

   * Table name → `iot_data`

   * Partition key → `basilbhai` (String)

   * Sort key → `id`　(String)

   * Capacity → `On-demand` for dev/test.

3. **Table 2**

   * Table name → `iot_HTTP_data`

   * Partition key → `part_id` (String)

   * Sort key → `id`　(String)

Both uploaded writer Lambdas already use those key names, so you can leave the code untouched.

---

## **2 · Import—and understand—the four Lambda functions**

### **2-A · Lambda-1 — write\_iot\_data-LambdaFunction.py (MQTT writer)**

Create **Lambda → Create function → “Author from scratch”**

* Name → `iotToDynamoWriter`

* Runtime → Python 3.12

* Execution role → `Create new role with basic Lambda permissions` (we’ll edit it in §3).

Paste the entire file:

write\_iot\_data-LambdaFunction.py

| import jsonimport boto3from datetime import datetimeimport uuiddynamodb \= boto3.resource('dynamodb')TABLE\_NAME \= 'iot\_data'table \= dynamodb.Table(TABLE\_NAME)def lambda\_handler(event, context):    try:        enriched\_payload \= event.copy()        slaveTime \= enriched\_payload.get('slaveTimestamp', enriched\_payload.get('timestamp', 0))        serverTime \= enriched\_payload.get('server\_timestamps', 0)        enriched\_payload\['RTT'\] \= serverTime \- slaveTime        for key in \['slaveTimestamp', 'server\_timestamps', 'masterReceiveTime',                    'masterSendTime', 'timestamp'\]:            enriched\_payload.pop(key, None)        enriched\_payload\['basilbhai'\] \= str(uuid.uuid4())       \# PK        enriched\_payload\['id'\]        \= str(uuid.uuid4())       \# SK        enriched\_payload\['ingestion\_time'\] \= datetime.timestamp(datetime.now())        item\_as\_strings \= {str(k): str(v) for k, v in enriched\_payload.items()}        table.put\_item(Item=item\_as\_strings)        return {'statusCode': 200,                'body': json.dumps(                   f"Data inserted into DynamoDB table '{TABLE\_NAME}' with ID "                   f"{item\_as\_strings.get('id', 'N/A')}")}    except Exception as e:        return {'statusCode': 500,                'body': json.dumps(f"Error inserting data into DynamoDB: {str(e)}")} |
| :---- |

### **2-B · Lambda-2 — write\_iot\_HTTP\_data-LambdaFunction.py (HTTP writer)**

Repeat **Create function**:

* Name → `httpToDynamoWriter`

* Runtime → Python 3.12

* Role → new role.

Paste the file:

write\_iot\_HTTP\_data[\-LambdaFunction.py](http://-LambdaFunction.py)

| import json, boto3, uuidfrom datetime import datetimedynamodb \= boto3.resource('dynamodb')iot\_client \= boto3.client(    'iot-data',    region\_name='ap-southeast-2',    endpoint\_url='https://a2xpqfvfr7pva7-ats.iot.ap-southeast-2.amazonaws.com')TABLE\_NAME \= 'iot\_HTTP\_data'table \= dynamodb.Table(TABLE\_NAME)def lambda\_handler(event, context):    try:        enriched\_payload \= {}        if event.get("queryStringParameters"):            enriched\_payload \= event\["queryStringParameters"\].copy()        elif "body" in event:            body\_content \= event\['body'\]            if event.get("isBase64Encoded"):                import base64                body\_content \= base64.b64decode(body\_content).decode('utf-8')            enriched\_payload \= (json.loads(body\_content) if isinstance(body\_content, str)                                else body\_content)        else:            enriched\_payload \= event.copy()        if {'server\_timestamp','slaveTimestamp'} \<= enriched\_payload.keys():            enriched\_payload\['RTT'\] \= (enriched\_payload\['server\_timestamp'\] \-                                       enriched\_payload\['slaveTimestamp'\])            enriched\_payload.pop('server\_timestamp', None)            enriched\_payload.pop('slaveTimestamp', None)        if 'message' in enriched\_payload:           \# echo back over MQTT if asked            iot\_client.publish(topic='esp32/sub', qos=1,                               payload=json.dumps(enriched\_payload).encode('utf-8'))        else:                                       \# persist            enriched\_payload\['part\_id'\] \= str(uuid.uuid4())            enriched\_payload\['id'\]      \= str(uuid.uuid4())            enriched\_payload\['ingestion\_time'\] \= datetime.timestamp(datetime.now())            item\_as\_strings \= {str(k): str(v) for k, v in enriched\_payload.items()}            table.put\_item(Item=item\_as\_strings)        return {'statusCode': 200,                'body': json.dumps(                    f"Data inserted into DynamoDB table '{TABLE\_NAME}' "                    f"with ID {enriched\_payload.get('id','N/A')}")}    except Exception as e:        return {'statusCode': 500,                'body': json.dumps(                    f"Error inserting data into DynamoDB: {str(e)} | Event: {event}")} |
| :---- |

### **2-C · Lambda-3 — read\_iot\_data-LambdaFunction.py (MQTT reader)**

Create **`getMqttData`**. Paste:

read\_iot\_data-LambdaFunction.py

| import boto3, jsondynamodb \= boto3.resource('dynamodb')table \= dynamodb.Table('iot\_data')def lambda\_handler(event, context):    try:        response \= table.scan()   \# tweak to Query if you add sort key        items \= response.get('Items', \[\])        return {'statusCode': 200,                'headers': {'Content-Type':'application/json',                            'Access-Control-Allow-Origin':'\*'},                'body': json.dumps(items)}    except Exception as e:        return {'statusCode': 500,                'body': json.dumps({'error': str(e)})} |
| :---- |

### **2-D · Lambda-4 — read\_iot\_HTTP\_data-LambdaFunction.py (HTTP reader)**

Create **`getHttpData`**. Paste:

read\_iot\_HTTP\_data-LambdaFunction.py

| import boto3, jsondynamodb \= boto3.resource('dynamodb')table \= dynamodb.Table('iot\_HTTP\_data')def lambda\_handler(event, context):    try:        response \= table.scan()        items \= response.get('Items', \[\])        return {'statusCode': 200,                'headers': {'Content-Type':'application/json',                            'Access-Control-Allow-Origin':'\*'},                'body': json.dumps(items)}    except Exception as e:        return {'statusCode': 500,                'body': json.dumps({'error': str(e)})} |
| :---- |

You now have **four** working Lambda functions—two writers (ingest) and two readers (query).

---

## **3 · Craft least-privilege IAM roles (5 min)**

Open **IAM → Roles → `role-iotToDynamo`** (the role linked to Lambda-1) → **Add inline policy** → **JSON**:

| {  "Version": "2012-10-17",  "Statement": \[    {      "Sid": "PutItemMqtt",      "Effect": "Allow",      "Action": "dynamodb:PutItem",      "Resource": "arn:aws:dynamodb:REGION:ACCOUNT:table/iot\_data"    }  \]} |
| :---- |

Add **AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole** managed policy for logs.

Create a second role **`role-httpToDynamo`** with `iot_data` → `iot_HTTP_data` switch and **iot:Publish** permission (topic `esp32/sub`) because Lambda-2 occasionally publishes back.

Roles for **reader Lambdas** need only **dynamodb:Scan** (or `Query`) on their respective tables.

---

## **4 · Expose the four API Gateway HTTP APIs (writers \+ readers)**

### **4-A · API-1 “write” endpoint (MQTT path)**

1. **API Gateway → Create API → HTTP API (cheaper)**

2. **Name** → `MQTTIngestAPI`

3. **Route** → `POST /ingest`

4. **Integration** → Lambda → Select **`iotToDynamoWriter`**

5. **Auth** → `NONE` (internal only)

6. **Create** and note the **Invoke URL** (copy to clipboard).

### **4-B · API-2 “write” endpoint (HTTP path)**

Repeat, name it `HTTPIngestAPI`, route `POST /ingest`, integration **`httpToDynamoWriter`**.

### **4-C · API-1 “read” endpoint**

In **`MQTTIngestAPI`** click **Routes → Add route**:

* `GET /data` → Integration \= **`getMqttData`**  
   Deploy.

### **4-D · API-2 “read” endpoint**

In **`HTTPIngestAPI`** add `GET /data` → **`getHttpData`**.

You now have **four HTTPS endpoints**:  
 *POST* `/ingest` (2 ×) for writes, *GET* `/data` (2 ×) for reads.

---

## **5 · Define the IoT Core Rule ⟶ Lambda-1 glue**

1. **IoT Core → Act → Rules → “Create rule”**

2. **Name**: `forwardEsp32ToLambda`

3. **Rule query statement**:

SELECT \* FROM 'esp32/pub'

4. **Add action** → *Invoke Lambda* → choose **`iotToDynamoWriter`** → “Create role” when asked.

That’s it—any message the ESP32 publishes lands inside Lambda-1, which persists it by calling DynamoDB.

---

## **6 · Deploy the HTTP producer on EC2 (server.js)**

SSH into your EC2 instance, install Node.js 18 or newer, then:

mkdir \~/iot-http && cd \~/iot-http  
nano server.js    \# copy-paste below  
npm init \-y && npm i express  
node server.js

Paste **server.js** exactly:

server.js

| const express \= require('express');const app \= express();const PORT \= 3000;app.use(express.json());app.post('/data', (req, res) \=\> {  const receivedData \= req.body;  console.log('Received data:', receivedData);  res.json({ message: 'Data received successfully', data: receivedData });});app.listen(PORT, () \=\> {  console.log(\`Server running on http:*//localhost:${PORT}\`);*}); |
| :---- |

Change your IoT device or any HTTP client to **POST** JSON to `http://<EC2-IP>:3000/data`.  
 Inside that route you typically add a `fetch()` or `axios.post()` call that forwards to **`https://<HTTPIngestAPI>/ingest`** so data hits Lambda-2; for brevity I leave that as an exercise.

---

## **7 · End-to-end smoke test (10 min)**

| Step | Tool | Expected |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| 1 | **IoT Core → MQTT test client** → publish:`{"temp": 24.3, "slaveTimestamp": 123, "server_timestamps": 456}` on topic `esp32/pub` | CloudWatch logs in **iotToDynamoWriter** show `statusCode 200`; **DynamoDB → iot\_data → Explore items** displays one row with a fresh `RTT` |
| 2 | On EC2 run:`curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"temp":29}' https://<HTTPIngestAPI>/ingest` | CloudWatch logs in **httpToDynamoWriter** show insert; **iot\_HTTP\_data** now has a row |
| 3 | Browser → `GET https://<MQTTIngestAPI>/data` | JSON array of mqtt rows (CORS \*) |
| 4 | Browser → `GET https://<HTTPIngestAPI>/data` | JSON array of http rows |

---

## **8 · Security hardening checklist (bookmark)**

| Area | Quick win |
| ----- | ----- |
| **API authorizers** | Switch the four API routes from “NONE” to IAM, Cognito JWT or Lambda authorizer once internal dev is over |
| **IoT policy security** | Attach the IoT certificate a policy that allows only `iot:Publish` on `esp32/pub` and `iot:Subscribe` on `esp32/sub` |
| **DynamoDB encryption** | Tables are encrypted by default; enable point-in-time recovery for oh-no moments |
| **VPC-only Lambda** | Place the writer Lambdas in a private subnet if you never call external HTTPS; they still reach DynamoDB via the VPC endpoint |
| **Least privilege roles** | Scan vs Query: if you add sort keys (`timestamp`), change reader Lambdas to `Query` so you can tighten IAM (`dynamodb:Scan` not required) |

---

### **You now own a four-Lambda / four-API pipeline**

* **MQTT branch**

  * ESP32 **publishes** ➜ *IoT Rule* ➜ Lambda-1 (`iotToDynamoWriter`) ➜ **DynamoDB iot\_data**

  * Browser **GET** ➜ API-1 `/data` ➜ Lambda-3 (`getMqttData`) ➜ **DynamoDB scan** ➜ JSON

* **HTTP branch**

  * EC2 **POSTs** ➜ API-2 `/ingest` ➜ Lambda-2 (`httpToDynamoWriter`) ➜ **DynamoDB iot\_HTTP\_data**

  * Browser **GET** ➜ API-2 `/data` ➜ Lambda-4 (`getHttpData`) ➜ **DynamoDB scan** ➜ JSON

All the code you supplied is in production with no CLI, no third-party libs, and full CORS-enabled reader endpoints.  
 Happy shipping\!

# Mil12 \- HTTP with EC2

***can we do this overall project on node js***   
***￼***  
***￼***  
***Setting up EC2***

So first of all click on instances. 

Then click on the launch instances button.

Then set the instance name and the machine OS which in our case we did this:  
![][image8]

After this click on create a new key pair, keep these settings of it:

![][image9]

Click on create key pair and then download it, that would not be used in our scenario but could be used in cases of remote access to this instance.

Then make sure to keep these settings:

![][image10]

Keep other settings as it as and then click on launch instance button located towards bottom right.

*Below we will Configure the EC2 instance through the terminal on AWS itself (accessing on AWS hence we don't need the key pair which we downloaded, that would be needed when we need to access it using our PC’s terminal). Follow below steps:*

So we had the jazzel repository which included the [node.js](http://node.js) and Server code which we had to deploy on our instance of EC2. 

Once a EC2 instance is launched then we need to below things first of all

1) Once created the instance, go to left there would be option of instances, click it  
2) Then after coming on the instances window, click on the instance ID which is highlighted in blue. Click on it.  
3) Then the instance summary would open, on top right after heading of the instance summary there would be the option of “connect”, click on it.  
4) Then the connect screen would load asking for whether to connect using public IP or private IP. choose the public IP option here.  
5) Then click on the connect option given below.  
6) After clicking on it a new screen would load, which would be a black screen called a terminal, which is like CMD of the windows and allows you to access and run codes on the remote instance of EC2 allocated to you.  
7) On it first of all run these commands: 

**Sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade**

**Sudo apt install npm**

Then run these commands: 

**Sudo apt install git (if git is not installed previously)**

**git clone [https://github.com/Jazzel/AWS-ESP32-Server.git](https://github.com/Jazzel/AWS-ESP32-Server.git)**

Ok so now we have cloned the repository of the server which we would need along with the dependencies mentioned which would be needed inside the “package.json” file which is included in this repository which we cloned.

Now for the next step run these commands:

1) **cd AWS-ESP32-Server**

2) **npm install**

**This command would install all dependencies which are mentioned in the package.json file.**

3) **npm start** 

**Now after running this command our server would start**

Now copy the public IP coming below of the instance. This IP would be used in next processes when integrating API’s.

More important steps are below to allow inbound traffic towards the EC2 instance from the 3000 port. Follow these steps:

1) Click on instances on left  
2) Then click on instance ID of your instance.   
3) Then click on the Security option below there  
   ![][image11]  
     
4) Then click on the security groups link shown in blue. The one with the wizard etc.  
5) After clicking on it this would load:   
   ![][image12]

Here click on edit inbound rules and allow custom TCP on the 3000 port. Thats it now the traffic can reach the EC2 server which we deployed.

***If you are done with the EC2 setup mentioned above then just follow these steps to start the server.*** 

Click on instances option on EC2 where it will show all instances deployed:  
![][image13]

From here see on the instance you just created and click on the instance id which is highlighted in blue, then this screen would load:   
![][image14]

Click on the connect option.

![][image15]

Now click on the connect option here again, this would bring you here:

![][image16]

Now follow these commands only:

1) **cd AWS-ESP32-Server**  
2) **npm start** 

That’s it, this would start the server now.

# Mil13-SNS+cloudwatch setup

**Configuring logging on IoT core**

First of all visit to this AWS IoT core, on left side scroll down to option of settings: 

**![][image17]**

Here click on settings, then this screen would load:   
**![][image18]**

Here click on ‘manage logs’ option in the logs area. Then this screen would be shown:

![][image19]  
Here please some info regarding this role, why it is needed etc:

* **Purpose**: The IAM role grants AWS IoT permission to send logs to CloudWatch on your behalf.  
* **Why Required**: AWS services need explicit permissions to interact with other services (like CloudWatch).  
* **What It Does:**  
  * Allows AWS IoT to create log groups/streams and write logs.  
  * Contains a trust policy (letting AWS IoT assume the role) and CloudWatch Logs permissions.  
* **Best Practice:** Use the "Create role" button for auto-configured permissions.

Now here what we need to do next is that click on the create role button:  
![][image20]

Keep some name of the role that you are making like this:   
![][image21]

Then click on create, then this screen would show: 

![][image22]  
There are various log levels available which are actually the level and intensity of logging that you need, in our case we chose ‘informational’ , so make sure to select that level.  
Once selected the **log level** now click on update. Then this screen would be shown: 

![][image23]

This shows that our role has been created and says logging has been successfully updated. 

Now after following these steps we have made sure that the cloudwatch will have log groups for IoT core too. This is all the setup which we had to do from the IoT core side. 

Scroll down for configuring AWS SNS.

**Configuring AWS SNS for Notifications**

First of all go to AWS SNS by searching in the search panel for ‘sns’. Once visited SNS then this screen would be shown: 

![][image24]

Here choose the topic name that you want to keep for the SNS topic. Like here I have kept a tutorial\_topic as name of my SNS topic. Then click on next step. Then this screen would be shown.   
![][image25]

Here select the standard. And below that all settings are optional which we did not     explore yet. So just keep scrolling down and click on **create topic** button.

Then this screen would be shown: 

![][image26]  
Here then click on **create subscription.** By creating subscription(s) we are actually giving an endpoint like phone number or email address to our SNS topic where it will direct the notifications. Showing configuration for adding a phone number as subscriber. Remember that whenever you create a new topic you will have to add subscriber(s) again. **After clicking on create subscription then this screen would load:**

**![][image27]**  
Here you dont need to be concerned about the topic ARN, it is configured automatically. You just need to select the protocol through which the notification would be sent, depending on your endpoint. Since we are doing for phone number then select SMS.   
![][image28]

Now you will have to select the endpoint, here it will only show endpoints that you have configured previously, if not then click on the add phone number option shown in this above picture and it will take you to another screen where you will have to add your phone number and a OTP would be sent to that number you will have to just enter it, this is simply AWS verification nothing else. Once adding the number there come back on this above same screen and then click on **“Create subscription”** button.  
![][image29]  
Now by doing so we have configured the endpoint of our SNS and the SNS topic itself too. 

**Configuring CloudWatch:**

First of all go to AWS cloudwatch then this screen would show, here click on in alarms option on left: 

![][image30]

After clicking on in alarm option this would show: 

![][image31]

Here click on create alarm

Then this screen would be shown:   
![][image32]

Here click on **Select metric** option. Then this screen would be shown: 

![][image33]

From here click on IoT shown in pic above: 

Then after clicking on IoT this would be shown: 

![][image34]

From here select the option rule metrics.

![][image35]  
Then this would be shown from here select the rule name which is the latest one that you configured. These are AWS IoT Core Rules that have been created in your AWS account. **IoT Rules are used to process and route messages from IoT devices**. Each rule shown here represents a different message processing rule:

* DemoRule \- A demonstration/example rule  
* EchoToRTT \- Routes messages for round-trip time testing  
* RepublishToEcho \- Republishes messages to Echo topics  
* RTT \- Measures round-trip time  
* rtt\_lambda \- Processes RTT data through Lambda functions

So based on your configuration select the rule which is best suit to you.

Once selected the rule then this screen will be shown :   
![][image36]  
From here: 

### **Namespace: AWS/IoT**

* This tells CloudWatch which AWS service the metric comes from  
* AWS/IoT \= metrics from AWS IoT Core service  
* You don't change this, it's automatically set

### **Metric name: TopicMatch**

* This is the specific measurement being tracked  
* "TopicMatch" counts how many times messages match your IoT rule  
* Think of it like a counter that goes up each time a message matches

### **RuleName: rtt\_lambda**

* This specifies which IoT rule you're monitoring  
* "rtt\_lambda" is the name of your specific IoT rule  
* This was selected from your previous screen

### 

### **Statistic: Average**

* This determines how CloudWatch calculates the metric over time  
* **Average** \= average number of matches per period  
* **Sum** \= total number of matches per period  
* **Maximum** \= highest number of matches in the period

### **Period: 5 minutes**

* How often CloudWatch checks the metric  
* "5 minutes" \= CloudWatch looks at your data every 5 minutes  
* Shorter periods \= more frequent checks, more sensitive alarms

Scroll down then this screen will be shown: 

![][image37]

## 

## For this setting read this below properly:
